title: Tonys Lady

jokes.text: Hey, beautiful co-workers. What a day it is to be young, gifted and Vic.
jokes.setup: Latka has multiple personality disorder and is currently being the slimy personality 'Vic'
jokes.punchline: Plays on 'young, gifted and black' but substitutes his own name with complete narcissistic confidence
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay that perfectly captures Vic's sleazy confidence while referencing the famous song. Character-specific humor that only works with Latka's condition.

dialogue.speaker: Latka/Vic
dialogue.line: You know what they say — young, gifted, and Vic.

jokes.text: You know, I'm getting used to Latka with these multiple personalities, but when he's Vic, he's such a slimy lounge lizard.
jokes.setup: Acknowledgment of Latka's condition
jokes.punchline: 'Slimy lounge lizard' perfectly captures Vic's essence
jokes.explanation: Good character description with vivid imagery, sets up the running dynamic for the episode.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby Baccalieri
dialogue.line: 'Slimy lounge lizard' perfectly captures Vic's essence

jokes.text: I'm just getting sick and tired of that little foreign wimp, Latka Gravas.
jokes.setup: Vic is complaining about someone
jokes.punchline: He's literally complaining about himself - his other personality
jokes.explanation: Brilliant ironic moment where a character literally insults his other personality. The absurdity is perfectly earned through the show's established premise.

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: You know what? I'm sick of this guy. He's always making my life miserable, contradicting everything I say, making stupid decisions...

dialogue.speaker: Vic (other personality)
dialogue.line: Oh, here we go again with the complaints. Maybe if you weren't such a jerk, people would actually like you!

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: See? This is exactly what I'm talking about! He never listens to reason!

jokes.text: Would you please do me a favor? ... Why don't you take little Captain Crud aside sometime and teach him how to operate a rag, okay?
jokes.setup: Vic asking for help dealing with Latka
jokes.punchline: 'Captain Crud' as an insult name for Latka, plus the dismissive 'operate a rag'
jokes.explanation: Good character-specific insult that shows Vic's crude personality while maintaining the absurdity of self-hatred.

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: Listen, I need your help dealing with Latka.

dialogue.speaker: Vic
dialogue.line: That Captain Crud can't even operate a rag.

jokes.text: What are you going to be, a door-to-door simpleton?
jokes.setup: Tony announces he got a new job
jokes.punchline: Louie's characteristically cruel but creative insult
jokes.explanation: Perfect Louie line - creative, mean-spirited, and economical. 'Door-to-door simpleton' is an inspired insult construction.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A new job? That's great, Tony. What is it, professional accident?

jokes.text: So what's the new job? I'm gonna be a chauffeur.
jokes.setup: Tony explains he's tired of driving a cab all day
jokes.punchline: His solution is to become a chauffeur - another driving job
jokes.explanation: Classic ironic setup-punchline structure. Tony's obliviousness to the contradiction is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm tired of driving a cab all day.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: So what are you gonna do?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Become a chauffeur.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Tony, that's still driving.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, but in a nicer car.

jokes.text: Talk about the Renaissance man.
jokes.setup: Tony's 'career change' from cab driver to chauffeur
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic comment about the minimal difference between the jobs
jokes.explanation: Good follow-up line that amplifies the irony with educated sarcasm.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: So Tony, you're going from being a cab driver to a chauffeur. That's quite a career move.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, it's a whole new life for me.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Right. Because the only difference is one guy yells at you through the partition, and the other one yells at you through the intercom.

jokes.text: That's great, Banta. You be nice to her, and maybe she'll let you walk her poodles. Be real nice to her, maybe she'll let you eat with them.
jokes.setup: Louie's first insult about walking poodles
jokes.punchline: Escalates to eating with the dogs, implying Tony is lower than pets
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie escalation - starts mean and gets meaner. The progression from servant to pet companion works well.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You're walking poodles? What are you, some kind of dog walker now?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: It's a job, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: A job? I've seen lower life forms. In fact, I have dinner with them every night — they're called my family. But you, you're eating WITH the dogs now?

jokes.text: Oh, my! What a surprise. Grease and oil in an engine? Are my eyes deceiving me?
jokes.setup: Alex asks Latka about his dirty engine
jokes.punchline: Latka's sarcastic shock at finding normal engine components
jokes.explanation: Good sarcastic response that shows Latka's mechanical knowledge while dismissing Vic's complaint.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, your engine is really dirty.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Dirty? What do you mean dirty? I look inside and what do I find? Normal engine parts! Can you believe it?

jokes.text: Listen, Vic is a fussbudget and a pain in the yaktabe.
jokes.setup: Latka responding to Alex about Vic's complaint
jokes.punchline: Latka insults his own other personality with a made-up foreign curse word
jokes.explanation: Another personality-vs-personality joke with Latka's characteristic foreign expressions. 'Yaktabe' is a memorable made-up insult.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Oh, Vic is always complaining. You know what his problem is? He's a putz!

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: No, no, no - I mean, my OTHER self, he's the real putz!

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Ah, go take a mud bath, you meshuga drek!

jokes.text: Mrs. Longworth? Miss. Oh, you can call me Christina. And you're? Very grateful.
jokes.setup: Standard introduction between employer and employee
jokes.punchline: Tony's overwhelmed response to meeting an attractive woman
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony response - genuine, awkward, and endearing. Shows his character through nervous attraction.

dialogue.speaker: Christina
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Christina. I'm new here.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Oh wow, hi! Welcome! I'm Tony. I'm... I'm the dispatcher here. Welcome to Taxi.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Can I get you anything? Coffee? Donut? My phone number?

jokes.text: If there is, I can't see it from here.
jokes.setup: Christina asks if something is wrong
jokes.punchline: Tony's honest admission that he's too smitten to notice problems
jokes.explanation: Economical and honest line that captures Tony's infatuation without being crude.

dialogue.speaker: Christina
dialogue.line: Is something wrong?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Wrong? No, nothing's wrong. I'm too smitten to notice.

jokes.text: And which do you prefer, uh, Christina or Peggy? Tony, Peggy is a nickname for Margaret. My nickname is Tina.
jokes.setup: Tony tries to be polite about name preferences
jokes.punchline: His complete confusion about how nicknames work
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's nervousness through his confusion about basic social conventions.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, some people, they don't like to be called by their nicknames. They prefer their real names.

dialogue.speaker: Christina
dialogue.line: That's very considerate of you, Tony.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, I figure if somebody's name is Robert, and they want to be called Robert, not Bob, you gotta respect that.

dialogue.speaker: Christina
dialogue.line: Absolutely.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So I says to this guy, 'What do you want me to call you?' And he says, 'My name is Robert.' So I say, 'Okay, but what's your real name?'

jokes.text: Except my brother... who calls me collect.
jokes.setup: Christina explaining that everyone calls her Christina except her brother
jokes.punchline: Her brother calls her collect (on the phone), not by a nickname
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay with perfect setup and misdirection. Economical and clever family relationship humor.

dialogue.speaker: Christina
dialogue.line: Everyone calls me Christina except my brother.

dialogue.speaker: Christina
dialogue.line: He calls me collect.

jokes.text: Hey, you, suck eggs! I'm sorry. I mean, uh... I mean, uh... I mean, exercise some caution, will you, fellow?
jokes.setup: Tony's instinctive cab driver response to another driver
jokes.punchline: His attempt to cover with overly formal language
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's struggle to be classy while his natural instincts take over.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Hey, watch where you're going, you moron!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I mean... kindly exercise more caution in your vehicular navigation, sir.

jokes.text: No, no, I can't see nothing from here. Yeah, but I met some real important people. Well, I don't want to brag, Alex. Well, but just to mention a few... The governor's chauffeur, the mayor's chauffeur.
jokes.setup: Tony talks about meeting important people at a classy party
jokes.punchline: The 'important people' are other chauffeurs
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that perfectly captures Tony's naive perspective and social status confusion.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I went to this real classy party, you know, met all these important people.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Turns out they were all chauffeurs.

jokes.text: What will you do when you ask me to leave and I don't? What, then, jerk? I don't know. I guess I'd break your face.
jokes.setup: Aggressive passenger challenges Tony's authority
jokes.punchline: Tony's matter-of-fact response about violence
jokes.explanation: Tony's casual, almost confused delivery makes the threat both funny and effective.

dialogue.speaker: Passenger
dialogue.line: You think you're tough? I could take you with one hand!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, I could take you with both hands tied behind my back.

dialogue.speaker: Passenger
dialogue.line: Oh yeah?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Yeah. But I wouldn't need 'em.

jokes.text: That settled it.
jokes.setup: Doug gets out of the car after Tony's threat
jokes.punchline: Tony's satisfied, simple assessment
jokes.explanation: Perfect understated reaction that shows Tony's practical approach to conflict resolution.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: That's what I like about Doug. He listens.

jokes.text: Oh, I wasn't worried. I mean, size isn't nearly as important as ability. You know, I was a professional boxer. I've been beat up by guys half his size.
jokes.setup: Tony explains why he wasn't worried about the bigger man
jokes.punchline: His boxing experience is getting beaten up by smaller opponents
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misdirection that subverts expectations about Tony's boxing prowess while staying perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: His boxing experience is getting beaten up by smaller opponents

jokes.text: I win on a TKO. Manzo can't come out of his corner 'cause I'm making him nauseous. That was a joke.
jokes.setup: Tony tells a boxing story about winning
jokes.punchline: He has to explain that the nausea line was a sportswriter's joke about his appearance
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor where Tony doesn't quite understand humor himself, adding another layer to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I won the fight, see? The ref raised my hand, the crowd went wild. Next day, the sportswriter says, 'Bianchi looked so bad, the crowd was nauseous.'

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So I'm telling this to the guys, and they don't get it. I gotta explain — he wasn't saying I made 'em sick from the fight, he was saying I'm so ugly, looking at my face made 'em nauseous!

jokes.text: You know, in his prime, he was considered one of the best middleweights of all time. But by the time I fought him, he was 43. I mean, his reflexes were off, his timing was bad, his punches were weak. With a couple of breaks, I might have beat him. That one was serious.
jokes.setup: Tony building up another boxing story
jokes.punchline: Even fighting a washed-up former champion, he only 'might have' won
jokes.explanation: Continues the pattern of Tony's boxing 'achievements' being less impressive than they initially sound.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So I'm in the ring, right? This guy, he used to be a champion. But now he's all washed up, you know? I mean, the guy can barely stand.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'm throwing punches, I'm moving around, I'm doing everything right. And you know what? I might have won that fight.

jokes.text: How about that? All these years of boxing finally paid off. I have amusing anecdotes.
jokes.setup: Tony reflecting on his boxing career
jokes.punchline: The only benefit from years of boxing is having stories to tell
jokes.explanation: Perfect summation of Tony's boxing career - self-aware humor about finding purpose in failure.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: The only benefit from years of boxing is having stories to tell

jokes.text: These could've been the greatest dates of my life if they had been dates. I'm never going to have better dates than these, and I never had these.
jokes.setup: Tony lamenting his professional relationship with Christina
jokes.punchline: Logical but absurd reasoning about hypothetical perfect dates
jokes.explanation: Tony's twisted logic about missed opportunities creates both humor and pathos.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You know, Christina and I, we could never work out. We're just too different.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I mean, think about it. If we went on a perfect date — she'd be happy, I'd be happy, we'd have a great time together — that would prove we're completely incompatible!

jokes.text: 'Aw, Tony'? You understood that? No. But he looks so sad.
jokes.setup: Alex expresses sympathy for Tony in confusing language
jokes.punchline: Latka admits he didn't understand but responded to emotion
jokes.explanation: Sweet moment showing Latka's empathy transcending language barriers.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I was trying to tell Tony how much I sympathized with him, you know, how I really felt for the guy.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I didn't understand a word you said.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You didn't?

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: No, but I could tell by the way you were talking that you really meant it, so I felt bad too.

jokes.text: She's the classiest lady I ever met. She's like a princess, she's like a goddess, she's like a Vegas showgirl.
jokes.setup: Tony describing Christina's class
jokes.punchline: List escalates from refined to... Vegas showgirl
jokes.explanation: The escalation reveals Tony's confused understanding of class and sophistication.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: She takes this class, you know, to learn how to be more refined. They teach her how to walk, how to talk, how to dress. By the end of the course, she's supposed to be this elegant, sophisticated woman.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So I ask her, 'How's the class going?' She says, 'Great! I learned how to be a debutante, then a socialite, then a fashion model, then a showgirl in Vegas.'

jokes.text: When was the last time you ended an evening by telling your date to wax the car?
jokes.setup: Tony explaining why Christina sees him as just help
jokes.punchline: The practical reality of their employer-employee relationship
jokes.explanation: Good reality check that grounds Tony's romantic fantasies in practical workplace dynamics.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think she sees me as just help? That's because I am the help. I'm not her friend, I'm not her peer - I work for her. She pays me, I do what she says. That's the practical reality of an employer-employee relationship.

jokes.text: I once lived with a woman for three years before I realized I loved her. In fact, I lived with her for three years before I realized I lived with her.
jokes.setup: Jim's advice about love creeping up on people
jokes.punchline: Escalates to not even realizing he lived with someone
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that reveals Jim's characteristic obliviousness in an absurdist way.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Love creeps up on you. You don't even realize it's happening.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: One day you wake up and you're in love with someone.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Hell, I didn't even realize I was living with someone for three months.

jokes.text: What a woman. I might have married her. Why didn't you? I said I might have. I don't remember.
jokes.setup: Jim talking about potentially marrying this woman
jokes.punchline: His circular logic and memory problems
jokes.explanation: Continues Jim's spacey character through confused logic about his own life.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I'm thinking about marrying this woman.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Jim, you just met her last week.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I know, but she's perfect. We're gonna be so happy together.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: How do you know that?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Because I'm gonna marry her, and I only marry women I'm happy with.

jokes.text: That's unique, Tony. But for God's sake, go easy on her.
jokes.setup: Someone suggests boxing as a unique date idea
jokes.punchline: Warning Tony not to hurt his date while boxing
jokes.explanation: Callback humor that plays on Tony's boxing experience in a dating context.

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: Boxing? That's a great idea for a date!

dialogue.speaker: Other Character
dialogue.line: Just be careful not to hurt her.

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'll take it easy on her.

jokes.text: Oh, it was a beautiful spring day. I fell in love with him that afternoon. Yeah? How'd it turn out? Oh, not so good. I married him.
jokes.setup: Elaine describing a romantic memory
jokes.punchline: Marriage ruined the romance
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection about romantic expectations vs. reality of marriage.

jokes.text: Banta, this is touching a chord in my heart.
jokes.setup: Tony's romantic situation
jokes.punchline: Louie claiming to have heart or emotions
jokes.explanation: Ironic given Louie's typically heartless behavior, sets up his upcoming 'advice.'

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, Tony, I may be a lot of things - a cheap boss, a ladies' man, a man of questionable moral character - but I got a heart.

jokes.text: Oh, yeah, the prison guard.
jokes.setup: Louie mentions being in love with a sophisticated woman
jokes.punchline: Tony's immediate assumption about Louie's type
jokes.explanation: Quick character-based assumption about Louie's romantic history.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Sophisticated? Louie, the last woman you were with thought a bidet was a new kind of soft drink.

jokes.text: When she did a grand jete, it could make you cry.
jokes.setup: Louie describing his ballerina love interest
jokes.punchline: Louie using proper ballet terminology and showing sensitivity
jokes.explanation: Surprising moment of culture and sensitivity from Louie, subverting character expectations.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: She's a dancer, you know? A real ballerina. And I been reading up on all the technical stuff - the pirouettes, the arabesques, the whole nine yards.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I even went to see her perform last night. And let me tell you, watching her move across that stage... it was poetry in motion.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: That's why I'm being so sensitive about the whole thing. I know how important her art is to her. So I told her, 'Baby, you go out there and kill it with your grand jetés and your pas de deux, and I'll be in the audience giving you a standing ovation.'

jokes.text: I know a spot on a woman's body that turns her to jelly.
jokes.setup: Louie offering romantic advice
jokes.punchline: His crude approach to seduction
jokes.explanation: Returns Louie to character after the brief sensitive moment - typical crude approach.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You want to know the secret to romance? You gotta be direct. You see a broad you like, you go right up to her and you say, 'Hey, you. Yeah, you. How'd you like to go out with me?' None of this fancy talk, none of this flowers and candy. Just lay it on the line.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: And if she says no, you say, 'Your loss, sweetheart.' Then you move on to the next one. That's how you do it. Simple. Effective. No games.

jokes.text: Well, if that works so great for you, Louie, why aren't you constantly surrounded by beautiful women? Because it's not an easy spot to get to.
jokes.setup: Alex challenging Louie's seduction claims
jokes.punchline: Louie's practical excuse for his lack of success
jokes.explanation: Good setup-punchline exchange that deflates Louie's boasting with logical questioning.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You're telling me you actually seduced all those women?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Well, not exactly seduced. More like... I offered them a ride and they said yes.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's not seduction, Louie.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: I know. That's why I don't have any success. I'm a cab driver. Where am I gonna take 'em? To the airport?

jokes.text: Take off your right shoe, Nardo. Drop dead. See, I told you it was a tough spot to get to.
jokes.setup: Louie trying to demonstrate his seduction technique
jokes.punchline: The 'spot' is her foot, and Elaine's rejection proves his point
jokes.explanation: Clever payoff that reveals both the absurd 'technique' and proves Louie's excuse simultaneously.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: See, there's a spot on every woman. You find that spot, they're yours.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Oh really? And where's my spot?

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Right here.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: No!

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: See? I told you I'd find it.

jokes.text: It doesn't matter, I'm always having a luau.
jokes.setup: Tony asking about timing for dropping by Jim's luau
jokes.punchline: Jim's perpetual luau lifestyle
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim line - absurd, character-specific, and economically delivered.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: So when's a good time to drop by your luau?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Oh, anytime. I'm having a luau.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You're always having a luau?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, I'm always having a luau. It never stops. I just keep it going.

jokes.text: ♪ Here I come to save the date. ♪
jokes.setup: Someone arriving to help Tony with dating advice
jokes.punchline: Parody of 'Here I come to save the day' but for dating
jokes.explanation: Simple wordplay that works but isn't particularly memorable or character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Character entering
dialogue.line: Here I come to save the day! I'm gonna help you with your dating problems, Tony!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Great, because I really need it.

jokes.text: You use this little goodie, and I promise it'll be full speed ahead from 'Can I buy you a drink?' to 'How do you like your eggs?'
jokes.setup: Offering Tony a pickup line
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic description of seduction progression
jokes.explanation: Decent pickup line humor with clear progression from meeting to morning after.

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Tony, I'm gonna give you a pickup line that never fails. You walk up to a beautiful woman and you say, 'If you were a vegetable, you'd be a cute-cumber.'

dialogue.speaker: Tony Banta
dialogue.line: That's terrible!

dialogue.speaker: Louie De Palma
dialogue.line: Terrible? I've used that line a hundred times. First she laughs, then you buy her a drink, then you suggest dinner, then you invite her back to your place to see your etchings, then you show her your bedroom, then you show her your... collection of rare coins.

jokes.text: No, no, no, Tony, you don't need any lines. I don't have any lines. Or any women, either, eh, Mahatma?
jokes.setup: Alex saying Tony doesn't need pickup lines
jokes.punchline: Someone points out Alex's own lack of success
jokes.explanation: Good comeback that undermines Alex's advice with his own romantic failures.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Tony doesn't need pickup lines. He's a very attractive man.

dialogue.speaker: Character
dialogue.line: Yeah, well neither do you, Alex. You need a pickup truck.

jokes.text: Yikes! This don't even rhyme. That's right, I was hoping it did.
jokes.setup: Tony reading Jim's poem and noting it doesn't rhyme
jokes.punchline: Jim's honest admission he was hoping it would
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim response showing his oblivious approach to poetry and general confusion about how things work.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: This doesn't rhyme.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I know. I was hoping it would.

jokes.text: How cold is your hand when not properly gloved... The sky is blue... And here comes the hardest part, Christina, which I like even better than Wheatina.
jokes.setup: Jim 'fixing' his poem to rhyme
jokes.punchline: Ridiculous forced rhymes including breakfast cereal
jokes.explanation: Absurd 'improvement' that makes the poem worse through Jim's misguided attempts at poetry.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, let me fix this to rhyme. 'Roses are red, violets are blue, I eat Frosted Flakes and so do you.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Wait, that doesn't work. How about... 'Roses are red, violets are Froot Loops, my love for you makes me do loop-de-loops.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: No, no... 'Roses are red, violets are Grape-Nuts, without you my heart is totally empty of cereal nuts.'

jokes.text: Christina, I can't go on with this charade anymore. I was wrong about everything, I'm sorry. I love you. I need you. Marry me. Oh, yes, Nick. Yes.
jokes.setup: Tony about to confess his feelings
jokes.punchline: He overhears Christina accepting another man's proposal
jokes.explanation: Perfectly timed dramatic irony that crushes Tony's hopes at the worst possible moment. High emotional impact.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Christina, I need to tell you something. I've been wanting to say this for a long time...

dialogue.speaker: Christina
dialogue.line: Oh Tony, that's so sweet, but I have something to tell you too.

dialogue.speaker: Man
dialogue.line: Christina, will you marry me?

dialogue.speaker: Christina
dialogue.line: Yes! Yes, I will!

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: ...I was just going to say your hair looks nice today.

jokes.text: Romance never works out. Only in fiction. I want to live in fiction. It wouldn't even have to be 'Wuthering Heights.' I'd settle for 'Nancy and Sluggo.'
jokes.setup: Lamenting that romance only works in fiction
jokes.punchline: Would settle for even comic strip romance
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from high literature to lowbrow comics, showing desperation for any kind of romantic success.

dialogue.speaker: Alex Reiger
dialogue.line: Romance only works in fiction. I'd settle for even comic strip romance.

jokes.text: The first time my heart was broken was in the ninth grade, and I got over it. A couple of years ago.
jokes.setup: Tony talking about getting over heartbreak
jokes.punchline: He only got over his ninth-grade heartbreak recently
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection that reveals Tony's romantic sensitivity and inability to let go of past hurts.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: He only got over his ninth-grade heartbreak recently

jokes.text: Well, we're going out tonight collecting aluminum cans. Crazy world.
jokes.setup: Jim describing his romantic date plans
jokes.punchline: The 'date' is collecting aluminum cans, Alex's understated reaction
jokes.explanation: Perfect ending that subverts romantic expectations with Jim's uniquely unglamorous but sincere approach to dating. Alex's 'crazy world' perfectly captures the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: I've got this great date planned. We're going to go out, spend some quality time together, really get to know each other.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: We're collecting aluminum cans.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Oh.

tmdb_overview: Tony takes a second job working as a chauffer for a wealthy but friendly woman named Christina. The two of them hit it off while he's driving her around, and Tony develops a crush on her. By the time Tony finally gets up the nerve to ask her out, it's too late-- she has just become engaged.
insight: Tony's romantic subplot anchors 46 jokes, but middling craft keeps this below Taxi's seasonal standard.
